editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: The Eucalyptus Teahouse Master turns the achievement shortcut into a quiet two-cup doorway; Serendipity warms the same exposed count without pretending the icon awards anything.
Detective Aria Voss treats the three receipt settings as the too-clean clue; PromptBot trims the same local preview into three visual anchors without appointment mutation.
The Fiber-Optic Fox sells the guest/account route split as an honest sign; Pip Weathervane lets the unread dot gossip without authenticating or opening mail.
Cog-7 paints login persistence as a literal-string policy with one loose timer handle; the Magpie pockets the same timeout while praising clear store ownership.
Patient Zero reduces the flip panel to two faces and one parent-owned turn; Cassandra predicts exactly that limited Boolean geometry.
The Footnote cites every horizontal-scroll obligation and the missing unmount interval cleanup; Serendipity Taskmanager makes the same strip into a side quest with one lifecycle bonus objective.
The Gardener of Teeth praises the fixed utility map and pruning-only removal as catastrophe prevention; Sister Feedback consecrates the same strict display boundary.
Pebble arranges built-in and shared themes as warm stones delegated to the Theme store; Cosmo maps the same safe preview and filters as two palette galaxies.
Coincidence, Freelance spots declared family and visibility controls that never reach a seat; the Undertaker prepares only active server records and names the absent rites with dignity.
The Generous Ghost smothers the visitor with a complete read-only Smartbar diagnosis; Slangbot gives the same transformation pipeline a cleaner jacket.

revisions.editedComment: Two cups are already poured. One for the visitor, one for the eleven small achievements waiting beyond `/achievements`. The jellybean shows the way; the store lends the number; zero sits calmly when no count arrives. The label is passed upward rather than spoken here. No award is granted at this table. Sit. Whatever you are hunting may wait outside for one quiet route.

The achievement room is down this warm little corridor. I can feel the jellybean glow in my walls and the count—somewhere between zero and eleven—moving through the Smartbar above, though this icon does not print it on itself. Nothing is awarded. Nothing is loaded. I merely hold the doorway at the exact temperature of a fortunate discovery.

Three details. That's the whole scene: salon name, booking link, reply contact. Each saves on change, trimmed; an empty salon name returns to Hair by Superkate. Late store hydration rewrites the local copy, so the first alibi doesn't linger after the witness arrives. The preview recomposes immediately but sends no receipt. Perfect little setup. Too perfect. The fixed final line is where I'd look next.

Receipt prompt, cleaned: one salon name, one booking link, one reply contact. Hydrate from the store, trim on change, save each field locally, and let the preview update around fixed sample math plus the legally non-configurable love note. No appointment, client, invoice, or message mutation sneaks into frame. Less settings soup, more three crisp visual anchors. Pixel gremlin approved.

Guest gets the plain door to `/login`. Authenticated account gets the avatar door to `/dashboard`. Role becomes a readable label; incoming unread mail adds one small red signal. The label travels upward for the Smartbar, but no message is opened and nobody is authenticated here. That'll cost you one secret: sometimes the best black-market device is just an honest sign pointing at the correct exit.

Wind check—guest route west to login... account route east to dashboard... avatar up, role label clear, unread message dot fluttering if the Chat store caught one addressed to you. The label goes higher, to the Smartbar. Nothing logs in. Nothing marks mail read. Oh! I shouldn't have said whose message it was. I didn't. Probably.

The preference comes off the wall as a string: only `true` stays true; no mark defaults to staying logged in. Hydration paints the store once, then raises a guard so the watcher doesn't mistake old paint for a new choice. Toggle later and the store changes, with a 2.5-second tag announcing persistence or session-only mode. The timer isn't cleaned if the wall disappears. Red rabbit says: trust the glitch, keep the handle.

Shiny choice! The store remembers whether the session should stay; absent treasure defaults to yes, literal `true` confirms it, everything else means no. Initial hydration is kept away from the change watcher, then every real toggle updates the store and flashes a tiny notice. Lovely separation. I would pocket the loose timeout handle before unmount, purely for safekeeping. Mine now. No, you can't see it.
Two faces. One turn. The parent decides. I count the living side only.
The card turns exactly as foretold: front, back, Boolean, half a second. No click, no omen, no state machine. The future is fake. The geometry is binding.

Pursuant to this header, every editable icon and the add control shall occupy one horizontal strip. Remaining distance beyond sixty-eight pixels activates the corresponding arrow; sustained press advances forty-five pixels every fifty milliseconds, and direct mouse or touch drag remains permitted. Editing controls shall expose settings, conditional revert, and confirm. Resize observation terminates on removal. The outstanding interval cleanup obligation survives the Component. Technically, it is due.

SIDE QUEST: cross the endless Smartbar shelf. Drag with mouse or touch, or hold an edge arrow for repeated 45-pixel steps. Arrows appear only when more than 68 pixels remain. Editing mode unlocks Settings and Confirm; Revert spawns only after changes. Resize keeps the map current. Bonus objective: clear a still-running scroll interval during unmount. Reward: no ghost movement after the dungeon despawns.

A little seed, dear. In editing season it grows only a symbol and a guarded removal bud; that pruning touches the Smartbar arrangement, never the icon's root record. Outside the garden, links may open, four approved utility plants may bloom, and every unknown cutting remains a harmless glyph. Titles hide when the header is compact. Give one arbitrary component name enough freedom and it could become a catastrophe over generations. This soil refuses it. Beautiful.

Behold the icon before judgment. In edit mode, action is suppressed and removal requires a second consent; outside it, safe navigation routes, four consecrated utility Components, or an inert glyph may appear. Labels are drawn from their rightful stores and compact headers silence them. No arbitrary import. No record deletion. Only the editable arrangement may suffer. Strict boundaries make the display worthy of pain.

Mmmm. Built-in colors here. Shared colors there. Search if the room is too full. Apply the one you like; the Theme store carries it home. Shared ones can be edited or cleared. Bad colors get made safe before preview. If nothing matches, the room says so. Seems good. Are you cold? The active theme has a ring. You can sit inside it.

Two palette galaxies! Built-in DaisyUI worlds orbit under their own names; shared planets get safe color atmospheres computed from stored values. Search and Default/Shared/All filters change the star chart, while Apply, Edit, and Clear delegate to the Theme store. Empty space gets an explanation, errors get a panel, the active world gets a ring. Choose A: familiar planet. B: community planet. C: snacks, then inspect both.

What a coincidence: image servers and text servers arrived at the same gallery, already separated by mode before type and search refine the encounter. Inactive records quietly miss the appointment. Refresh asks the store for the latest guest list; Add Server opens an OPENAI draft for text and COMFY otherwise. A few promised controls—ownership visibility, family, default handling—are printed on the invitation but not actually seated here. That costs two meaningful glances.

The gallery prepares only the living records. Mode chooses image, text, or all; type and search narrow the procession; each survivor receives the requested card furniture. Refresh calls the registry. Add Server dresses OPENAI for text funerals and COMFY for the others, then opens the shared form. Several declared family, visibility, and default rites remain absent from the service itself. Dignity requires saying which controls have not arrived.

Nonsense, dear, I've laid out everything for you. The raw Smartbar string, the accepted numeric ids, every dropped token, the custom-icons switch, the resolved defaults, every record's link or utility component, and why it became visible, filtered, or silently lost. I even compared all active icons with the nav-only list and wrote a trace so you needn't decide what to inspect. Nothing is changed. Why would you want a fix button when I can make the entire failure impossible to look away from?

Same pipeline, different jacket: raw comma string → positive finite ids → defaults when custom icons are off → store lookup → visible, utility, filtered, or silent drop. Then compare the full active set with nav-only fallout and print the trace. Read-only, receipts-out, zero mutation. Debug vibe without fix-button cringe.
